title: Soy wax tart melts
subtitle: Scented soy wax melts for use with an oil burner or electric wax melter
excerpt: Pour soy wax into a silicone tray mould with fragrance oil and leave to set. Each tart releases fragrance when warmed -- no flame involved if using an electric melter. A batch of 20 melts makes several weeks of daily use.
sourceNotes: Soy wax fragrance-load properties: standard candle-supply chemistry. Fragrance-oil load capacity for container soy wax: manufacturer guidelines for GW-464 grade soy wax.

recipe.batchNotes: Scale up freely. The standard fragrance load is 10% by weight of wax.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Soy wax melts are stable for a year at room temperature. Make a large batch and store.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Store at room temperature away from direct sunlight. Discard if the wax surface turns greasy or the fragrance smell is no longer detectable.

glossaryTerms.definition: Hydrogenated soybean oil wax with a melt point of about 50°C. Used for container candles and tart melts. Holds up to 8% fragrance by weight and gives a creamy-white finish.

content.text: Tart melts are small blocks of scented wax placed on the warming dish of an oil burner or electric wax melter. The gentle heat releases the fragrance without burning the wax.

is the standard choice: it sets to a smooth, creamy-white finish and holds fragrance well at 10% of wax weight. Use a candle-grade fragrance oil for the strongest throw, or an essential oil such as lavender or eucalyptus for a natural alternative.

items.prepNote: 10% of wax weight; or replace with 20 g essential oil

content.text: Place the soy wax in a candle pouring pitcher or a heatproof jug set inside a saucepan of simmering water (a double boiler). Melt over a medium-low heat. Monitor the temperature with a probe thermometer; remove from the heat when the wax reaches 65 to 70°C and is fully melted.

Allow the melted wax to cool slightly to 60°C, then stir in the fragrance oil. Stir slowly and steadily for 30 seconds to ensure full incorporation. Adding fragrance at the right temperature stops it from flashing off before the wax sets.

Pour the scented wax into a silicone mould -- a soap or chocolate mould with individual cells works well. Fill each cell to just below the rim. Leave undisturbed at room temperature for at least one hour. Do not move the mould while the wax is setting, as this causes sinkholes. Once set, pop the melts out of the mould.

Place one or two melts on the warming dish of an electric wax melter or in the top bowl of a tealight oil burner. The heat softens and melts the wax, releasing the scent. Remove the dish and allow to cool and re-solidify between uses. The same melt can be reused several times until the scent is exhausted. Dispose of spent wax in the bin -- do not pour melted wax down a drain.

Store in a sealed box or bag at room temperature, away from heat and direct sunlight. Use within one year. Discard if the surface of the wax looks sweaty or oily (fragrance oil has separated out), or if the scent is no longer detectable.
